sources: LCC Local Highways Maintenance Transparency Report (condition data)
LCC Cabinet reports — 2025/26 and 2026/27 capital programme (budgeted, not confirmed outturn)
LCC Cabinet Member Warren Goldsworthy statement — £650M backlog (March 2026)
AIA ALARM Survey 2024 — national backlog figures
DfT Road Condition Statistics RDC series — national context
CIPFA Transport Infrastructure Code — asset valuation methodology
meta.data_quality_note: Capital programme figures (2025/26, 2026/27) are budgeted allocations, NOT confirmed outturn spending. Revenue outturn data (2021/22–2024/25) is confirmed GOV.UK MHCLG data. Road condition data is from LCC's own DVS surveys.

network_summary.gross_replacement_cost_note: Per CIPFA Transport Infrastructure Code — the cost to replace the entire network as new. This is NOT a market value.
network_summary.depreciated_replacement_cost_note: DRC (GRC minus accumulated depreciation) not publicly published by LCC. If average asset age is 40% through life, DRC ≈ £6B.
network_summary.responsible_for: LCC is the highway authority for 12 Lancashire districts. Blackpool and Blackburn with Darwen are unitary authorities managing their own networks.

asset_categories.note: Surface and sub-base. The largest single asset class — 75% of total GRC.

asset_categories.quantity: Extensive network alongside 4,437 miles of road
asset_categories.note: Includes 5,300 miles of footpaths. Significant maintenance liability.
asset_categories.primary_treatment: Slurry seal, bitmac overlay, slab replacement

asset_categories.note: Bridges are complex, high-cost assets. Failures attract public/legal scrutiny. £6M A601M/A6070 Carnforth structures programme underway.
Column lifespan typically 30–40 years. LED conversion ongoing. Asset includes columns, lanterns, underground cable network.
asset_categories.primary_treatment: LED conversion, column replacement, cable upgrade

asset_categories.note: Includes signal heads, controllers, underground loops, SCOOT/MOVA systems. Increasingly connected/smart.
asset_categories.primary_treatment: Controller replacement, LED signal heads, comms upgrade

asset_categories.note: Often neglected until flooding occurs. Gully emptying is a high-volume routine task. SuDS becoming mandatory from 2025.
asset_categories.primary_treatment: Gully emptying, CCTV survey, pipe relining

road_condition.survey_method: Detailed Video Survey (DVS) — Lancashire's own system. Graded 1–5: Grade 4 (Functionally Impaired) and Grade 5 (Structurally Impaired) = Red.
road_condition.transition_note: From 2026/27, LCC transitions to BSI PAS 2161 — five categories instead of three. Historical comparisons will break at that point.

a_roads.note: Below national average (~4%). A roads receive most maintenance investment — preventative surface dressing keeps them in better condition.

bc_roads.note: B&C roads deteriorating faster than A roads — jumped from 4.59% to 6.49% in a single year (2023/24 → 2024/25). High rainfall 2024 (125% of 1991–2020 average) accelerated damage.

unclassified.note: Over 1-in-4 unclassified roads need structural maintenance. These are the streets residents actually live on. Unclassified accounts for 87.5% of the network by length.

trend.note: Post-lockdown — reduced traffic extended road life
Significant deterioration in B&C roads. High rainfall a major factor.

road_condition.key_insight: The unclassified network — where people live — has been at ~27–28% red for 5 consecutive years. This is a chronic problem, not a spike.

budget_trend.note: Revenue expenditure is CONFIRMED GOV.UK MHCLG outturn data. Capital programme (2025/26, 2026/27) are BUDGETED allocations — actual spend may differ. Budget ≠ delivery.

years.capital_note: £61M capital programme (budgeted). Included £27.9M structural defects, £19.7M resurfacing, £4.2M residential roads. DfT Band 3 allocation plus £15M additional LCC borrowing.

years.source: LCC Budget Book / Full Council Feb 2026
years.capital_note: £72M total: £48.5M DfT + £18.5M council budget + additional/carry-over. Approved by Full Council. BUDGETED — not confirmed outturn. Focus: resurfacing, structural repairs, bridges, drainage, street lighting.

investment_analysis.lcc_backlog_source: Cabinet Member Warren Goldsworthy, March 2026 budget debate
investment_analysis.lcc_backlog_note: This is the LCC-specific stated backlog — the cost to bring the entire network up to standard. At £72M/year capital spend, this would take ~9 years to clear if NO further deterioration occurred (which is impossible).
investment_analysis.national_backlog_note: This is the Asphalt Industry Alliance estimate. Some critics argue the methodology overstates the backlog; others argue it understates it. LCC's £650M is plausible — ~3.9% of the national figure for a network that represents a similar fraction.
investment_analysis.steady_state_concept: Steady state = the annual investment required to maintain current condition without further deterioration. NOT the same as clearing the backlog.
investment_analysis.steady_state_note: Rough estimate: £10B GRC ÷ 25-year average life = £400M/year. In practice, different asset types have very different lifespans (road surface 10–40yr, bridges 80–120yr, lighting 30–40yr). This £400M figure is illustrative only — it compares poorly to current spend.
investment_analysis.capital_as_pct_steady_state_note: Even the 2026/27 £72M capital programme is only ~18% of the estimated steady-state need. The network is depreciating faster than it is being renewed.
investment_analysis.pothole_commitment_note: £45M additional borrowing for pothole repair over 3 years (£15M/year). Approved at Full Council Feb 2026. This is a CAPITAL commitment, not revenue.
investment_analysis.key_insight: LCC faces a structural £650M backlog, is investing ~£72M/year in capital (18% of steady-state need), and the unclassified network has been at ~27-28% red for 5 consecutive years. Without a step-change in investment, the backlog will continue to grow. The DfT Band 3 incentive (25% funding held back until criteria met) creates additional pressure to demonstrate best practice asset management.

lifecycle_models.description: Spray bitumen binder and chippings over existing surface. Preventative — seals cracks and waterproofs.
lifecycle_models.suitable_for: A and B roads with good structural integrity
lifecycle_models.note: Most cost-effective per year of life. 58 miles treated in 2024/25 LCC programme.

lifecycle_models.description: Thin bitumen emulsion with fine aggregate. Fills surface texture, extends life.
lifecycle_models.note: Fills surface texture without structural repair — only appropriate if road structure is sound.

lifecycle_models.description: 15–50mm overlay of premium asphalt. Gives new surface without full depth repair.
lifecycle_models.note: 5-year guarantee typical. Only works where sub-base is sound.

lifecycle_models.description: Remove top 40–60mm and replace with new asphalt. Addresses surface and upper structural layer.
lifecycle_models.suitable_for: Roads with structural deterioration but sound sub-base
lifecycle_models.note: 24 miles resurfaced in 2024/25 LCC programme. Most common major treatment.

lifecycle_models.description: Complete removal and rebuild including sub-base. Addresses full-depth structural failure.
lifecycle_models.suitable_for: Severely deteriorated roads (Grade 5), roads with failed sub-base
lifecycle_models.note: 5× more expensive per year of life than surface dressing. Reactive repair (waiting until Grade 5) vs preventative is the core cost argument for early intervention.

running_costs_context.note: Revenue running expenses from GOV.UK confirmed outturn include: staff costs, contracts for routine maintenance, street lighting energy, winter service, drainage maintenance, structure inspections, traffic signal maintenance, and management overhead. The revenue budget does NOT include capital renewal — that is a separate capital programme.

latest_confirmed.income_sources: Primarily DfT highway maintenance grant plus developer contributions (s278/s38 agreements)

implied_running_cost_breakdown.basis: Estimated portion of running expenses — includes patching, reactive repairs, vegetation, drainage
Estimated — LCC grits ~2,000 miles of priority routes
Estimated — 2,009 structures need annual inspections and routine maintenance

running_costs_context.disclaimer: Running cost breakdown estimates are derived from national benchmarks applied to LCC scale. LCC does not publish a service-level breakdown of running expenses. Only the total employee/running expense/income split is confirmed.

innovation_opportunities.title: LED Street Lighting Conversion
innovation_opportunities.value_summary: 50–70% energy saving per column. Nationally £125M/yr electricity + £60M/yr maintenance savings.
innovation_opportunities.lancashire_angle: LCC's capital programme includes lighting column replacement alongside resurfacing. Older estate areas and rural roads likely still have sodium lanterns. Full conversion = significant energy saving.
innovation_opportunities.risk_note: Upfront capital cost. Some communities object to LED colour temperature. PFI/outsourced lighting contracts may complicate conversion timelines.

innovation_opportunities.title: Heritage Cobblestone & Sett Recovery
innovation_opportunities.value_summary: C18-C19 Yorkshire stone setts beneath tarmac in Lancashire mill towns. Potential heritage, tourism, and conservation area value.
innovation_opportunities.lancashire_angle: Burnley, Accrington, Colne, Nelson, and Clitheroe all have historic mill street networks. When roads are due for resurfacing, ground-penetrating radar could identify sett locations. Exposed setts on pedestrianised streets add character and reduce future resurfacing costs (setts outlast asphalt). Conservation area status may require retention. Some setts removed unlawfully in the 1970s–80s resurfacing wave.
innovation_opportunities.policy_driver: NPPF Chapters 16–17 (Heritage Assets), Historic England guidance, Conservation Area Appraisals
innovation_opportunities.risk_note: Sett streets have higher slip risk and are harder to navigate for cyclists/wheelchair users. Not appropriate for high-speed roads. English Heritage and local conservation officers must be consulted.

innovation_opportunities.title: Biodiversity Net Gain — Highway Verges
innovation_opportunities.value_summary: Highway verges as BNG habitat bank. Developers need 10% BNG — highway authorities could sell credits.
innovation_opportunities.lancashire_angle: Lancashire's 4,437 miles of roads come with substantial verge area. Rural verges in particular support rare grassland species. LCC could register verges as habitat bank sites under the statutory BNG framework, generating income from developer BNG credit purchases. Requires management plans and monitoring but could offset routine maintenance costs.
innovation_opportunities.policy_driver: Environment Act 2021 s98–101 (mandatory BNG from Feb 2024 for major development), DEFRA metric 4.0
innovation_opportunities.risk_note: Requires upfront survey and habitat baseline. Management must be maintained for 30 years. Complex monitoring requirements. Legal framework still evolving.

innovation_opportunities.title: 5G Small Cell Hosting on Street Furniture
innovation_opportunities.value_summary: Street lighting columns and signal poles hosting 5G equipment. Revenue via wayleave agreements with telecoms operators.
innovation_opportunities.lancashire_angle: ~100,000 lighting columns represent significant hosting infrastructure. DCMS 5G coverage targets mean operators are actively seeking street-level sites. Lancashire could negotiate a framework agreement with telecoms providers for wayleave rent on columns. Column-hosted small cells at 5–10m height ideal for dense urban coverage.
innovation_opportunities.policy_driver: Electronic Communications Code (ECC), Telecommunications Infrastructure (Relief from Non-Domestic Rates) Act 2018, DCMS 5G Strategy
innovation_opportunities.risk_note: Requires structural assessment of columns (load-bearing capacity). Community concerns about 5G on residential streets. Column ownership must be verified (some PFI-contracted). Revenue depends on operator demand — market still developing.

innovation_opportunities.title: Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS) Integration
innovation_opportunities.value_summary: SuDS typically cheaper over whole life than conventional piped drainage. Reduces flood risk and sewer overloading. Also delivers BNG and biodiversity benefits.
innovation_opportunities.lancashire_angle: Lancashire experienced 125% of average rainfall in 2024 — a direct cause of accelerated road deterioration. Permeable paving, drainage swales, and roadside rain gardens alongside resurfacing works would reduce subsurface water saturation (the primary cause of road failure) and reduce gully emptying frequency. Future-proofing against climate change.
innovation_opportunities.policy_driver: Flood & Water Management Act 2010 Schedule 3, CIRIA SuDS Manual C753, Environment Act 2021
innovation_opportunities.risk_note: Higher upfront construction cost than conventional drainage. Maintenance regimes for permeable surfaces need careful design. Requires coordination with drainage authorities and sewage operators.

innovation_opportunities.title: Subsurface Utility Mapping & Asset Register
innovation_opportunities.value_summary: Utility strikes cost £2.4B/year nationally (30% avoidable with better mapping). NUAR digital twin going live 2025.
innovation_opportunities.lancashire_angle: Every utility strike during roadworks causes delay, remediation cost, and potential road surface damage that accelerates deterioration. A comprehensive subsurface asset register (to PAS 128 standard) enables LCC to charge full Section 59 NRSWA costs for road damage, coordinate works earlier, and reduce reactive repairs. Lancashire has unexplored historical infrastructure — wartime aviation fuel pipelines, Victorian sewers, and pre-war drainage under many roads.
innovation_opportunities.policy_driver: PAS 128:2022, NRSWA 1991 s69 (Records), National Underground Asset Register (NUAR), Geospatial Commission strategy
innovation_opportunities.risk_note: Significant upfront survey investment. Data sovereignty and security concerns (utility companies protective of their asset data). Benefits accrue over many years — difficult to quantify short-term.

valuation_questions.intro: Road asset valuation in UK local government follows CIPFA's Transport Infrastructure Code, which mandates Depreciated Replacement Cost (DRC) methodology. The numbers are large — but are they right?

questions.title: Cobblestones: Hidden Heritage Asset?
questions.question: Are sett streets valued as standard carriageway (resurfacing cost) or as heritage assets with higher intrinsic value?
questions.analysis: CIPFA Code values carriageways at their engineering replacement cost. A sett-paved street would be valued at the cost of relaying granite setts — which is significantly MORE expensive than laying asphalt. However, conservation area designation and Listed Building context mean the heritage value (tourism, sense of place, conservation) is not captured in the CIPFA valuation. There is a theoretical argument that LCC's GRC is understated for heritage sett streets.
questions.implication: If LCC holds significant sett infrastructure under tarmac, and this is being valued as asphalt-equivalent, the heritage asset register may be understated.

questions.title: LED Lighting: Capital vs Revenue Classification
questions.question: LED conversion is sometimes classified as capital (asset upgrade) and sometimes as revenue (maintenance). Correct classification affects both balance sheet asset value and annual running cost reporting.
questions.analysis: Where LED conversion materially extends asset life and enhances capability, it should be capitalised under CIPFA Code. Revenue treatment (expensing it) improves the balance sheet appearance but may misrepresent the nature of the expenditure. LCC's lighting asset value may fluctuate depending on this classification decision.
questions.implication: Reclassifying LED conversion as capital would increase the infrastructure asset value on the balance sheet and reduce apparent running costs — but requires a proper componentisation policy.

questions.title: Drainage: Systematically Undervalued?
questions.question: Gully pots, pipes, headwalls, and culverts are often poorly inventorised and therefore potentially undervalued in the asset register.
questions.analysis: Unlike carriageways (surveyed by DVS) and bridges (statutory inspections), drainage assets are difficult to survey systematically. CCTV surveys are expensive and rarely comprehensive. This means the GRC for drainage is an estimate with higher uncertainty than other categories. If drainage assets are undervalued, total GRC understates the true replacement cost.
questions.implication: A systematic drainage survey programme (using CCTV/sonar where accessible) would improve asset data quality and likely increase total GRC. Better data also supports better prioritisation of proactive drainage maintenance.

questions.title: Highway Verges: Excluded from Asset Register?
questions.question: Are highway verges (the land strip between carriageway and boundary) included in the infrastructure asset register, and what is their true value?
questions.analysis: Under CIPFA, highway land (including verges) is part of the highway infrastructure asset. However, verge land value is often assigned a nil or nominal value because there is no market for it (it cannot easily be sold). Yet highway verges adjacent to development land may have significant development value — or BNG credit value. The 'nil value' treatment may understate the total asset portfolio.
questions.implication: A strategic review of verge land value — particularly at key junctions and development edges — could identify assets with unrecognised value.

legislation.duty: Duty to maintain adopted highways in a state of repair reasonably safe for ordinary traffic
Defence of reasonable maintenance — council must demonstrate it took all reasonable precautions to maintain
Principal road maintenance duty — A and B roads

standards.purpose: GRC/DRC asset valuation methodology — how highway assets are valued on the balance sheet
Certified system for road condition surveys and analysis — used for DfT data returns
New 5-category road condition standard replacing 3-category RED/AMBER/GREEN for national reporting
Replaces Well-Maintained Highways — sets out risk-based approach to maintenance prioritisation
Lifecycle planning toolkit — models funding impact on asset performance and calculates steady-state
Specification for underground utility detection, verification, and location — 4 quality levels for SUE surveys
